verdictSummary: Warner Bros sold audiences on a dark psychological villain story but delivered a musical sequel, creating a massive disconnect that left 64% of viewers giving negative reviews. The studio completely hid the musical element from their marketing campaign, leading audiences to feel confused and deceived about the film's actual nature. This explains the D CinemaScore and widespread disappointment among fans who came expecting another serious character study. The marketing failure wasn't about quality but about fundamentally misrepresenting what kind of movie this actually was.

quotes.text: This is the story of Arthur Fleck... and his descent into chaos.
I was promised a dark psychological thriller and got a Broadway musical. What happened?
The trailers showed intensity and madness. The movie gave us Lady Gaga singing for two hours.
Absolutely baffling creative decision to make this a musical.

diagnosis: Warner Bros sold audiences on a dark psychological villain story but delivered a musical sequel, creating a massive disconnect that left 64% of viewers giving negative reviews. The studio completely hid the musical element from their marketing campaign, leading audiences to feel confused and deceived about the film's actual nature. This explains the D CinemaScore and widespread disappointment among fans who came expecting another serious character study. The marketing failure wasn't about quality but about fundamentally misrepresenting what kind of movie this actually was.
recommendation: Release a secondary trailer highlighting the musical numbers and singing performances at least six weeks before opening day.
lostAudience: Fans of the original Joker expecting dark psychological content; general audiences who don't enjoy musicals.
what_was_promised: Warner Bros marketed Joker: Folie à Deux as a dark psychological thriller focusing on chaos, villainy, and the titular character's menacing power.
what_was_received: Audiences experienced a confusing musical sequel that left them bored and disappointed, feeling their time was wasted.
marketing_explanation: The marketing emphasized 'joker', 'chaos', 'villain', and 'psychological' themes while completely concealing the musical element. Audiences felt blindsided by discovering it was a 'musical' and expressed being 'confused' about what they had actually signed up to see.
lost_audience: Fans of the original Joker film who expected another gritty psychological character study were most betrayed by the undisclosed musical format.

This explains the D CinemaScore and widespread disappointment among fans who came expecting another serious character study. The marketing failure wasn't about quality but about fundamentally misrepresenting what kind of movie this actually was.
